CURRENT AFFAIRS.

The Evenwing T'elegraph’s Christmas shopping expert.
is busy just now. We have been keeping a casual eye
on him as he reveals himself in those excellent gems of
literature that.he contributes to Zin/k. For some days
it would appear as if he had the pledge, for we did not
notice any puff of a bungery ; indeed, we were wondering
if he had not turned a rabid teetotal advocate. But,
as the days went on, he dropped into a few bungeries.
On Wednesday, the 18th, he wrote up a bungery in
Great Britain Street, where ‘‘ all wines and whiskies
kept upon the premises are of a first-rate quality only,
and all those gettixg in a stock of suitable drinks for the
forthcoming festive times may be sure of receiving satis-
faction and attention at the well-known house in Great
Britain Street.”” If we may take it that he visits all
the places he puffs, and that his visits axe set dut in due
sequence in Pink, he visited a pawn-shop in FleotStreet,

. hefore going to the Great Britain Strect bungery. Of-

the pawn-shop he writes:—‘‘ To those who are hard

up and obliged to part at their festive time with some of
their carthly possessions a fair price will be given by
this well-established firm.”

Pink’s Christmas shop expert amuses us annually.
Some of his pufis read like bits of eloquent speeches ab
a village political meeting. Ile went into a hat shop
and declares: —‘“ To be well hatted is as important as
to be well clothed or shod, and the man must be hard
to please who will not be satisfied with the perfect style
and fit of the article supplied by this fashionable hatter.””
In fact he found on the premises ‘‘ gentlemen’s silk hats
and felt hats which are never felt so perfectly they fit.””
That is a gem in the art of Christmas puff writing. One
might compose a riddle out of it—when are felt hats not:
felt? Answer—When ‘‘ so perfectly they fit”’! From
the hat shop where felt hats are not felt Pink’s repre-
sentative dropped into—a brewery. Ie commences:—
““This firm of brewers has had a long and famous re-
cord, and still stands immovably in the front rank in
spite of all competition.”” At the end of the puff thig
Evening Telegraph special warns the public in this
fashion: —‘“ Be sure- when thirsty to ask only for
£ *.and to refuse to even look at, much less drink,
any other.”” Oh, Pink, Pink, we wonder what will you
say when you go to another brewery! Picture a
“bona,’” as a result of Pink’s advice, refusing even to
look at drink from another brewery!

Leaving the brewery the FEwvening Telegraph man
popped into a restaurant where ‘‘ the famous grill will
provide the hungry traveller with a perfect beef-steak,
and the varied supply of the choicest wines can be
selected to-allay his thirst.””  We trust that- Pink’s
special had a good beef-steak. Anyway, he proceeded
to an optician’s and he asks—‘‘ ITas not his reputation
for spectacles been established for countless generations
in Dublin.”” ‘“ Countless generations ! Steady, Pink,
steady now !

Mr. John Sweetman has written a letter on the situa-
tion to the Independent, and the Independent has it
that he ‘‘ states the case for the Sinn Feiners.”” Mr.
Sweetman is an amiable crank with plenty of money and
some land, who is against cattle-driving and also against
the British Empire, and who yet receives the Imperial
Income Tax collector, for, as he says, if he didn’t, his
furniture would be seized. Mr. Sweotman is a theorist,
and a man with plenty of money who does not mind
being laughed at can hold many absurd theories. A
man who has to earn his living has in many cases to be
careful of his reputation for common sense. If Mr.
Sweetman lived by the law, for instance, how many.
would say—if he is so outlandish and queer in his
political views, why should I give my case to him? Or,
if he were a medical doctor, how many would say—If
his diagnosis of politics is so peculiar, why should I
trust the fortunes of my liver to him ?

Mr. Sweetman says: —‘‘ England has no right to with-
lold from Ireland the power to govern herself, which is
tho inherent right of every nation.””  Of course, she
hasn’t; and, by a parity of reasoning, England has no
right to send hen Income Tax collector to Mr. Sweet-
man! But Mr. Sweetman has agreed to stand and
deliver to Mr. British Income Tax collector, for, as
he says, if he didn’t, his furniture would be seized!

Mr. Sweetman goes on:—‘‘ She does so, and Mr.
Birrell is her agent for that purpose. I think, there-
fore, no Irishman who claims the right of nationhood
for Ireland should have any political friendship with

irrell. Any Irishman who wishes to give what
call fair play to Mr. Birxell practically gives up the
mand for Irish nationhood, which no Irishman has a
ht to do.”” We suppose we are to take it that any
hman who holds that Ireland has an inherent right

govern herself and has, therefore, no right to give
‘f’xyha.t you call fair play to Mr. Birrell,”” for so doing
means his giving ‘‘up the demand for Irish nation-
“hood,”” is quite ab liberty to give a peaceful reception to
) - the British taxpayer and pay up his Income Tax with-
out prejudice to ‘‘ the demand for Irish nationhood ’’!
But Mr. Sweetman can withhold ‘“ what you call fair
- play to Mr. Birrell ” without risk to himself, but if he
withheld his Income Tax, well, then, his furniture would
be seized !

Mr. Sweetman proceeds:—‘‘In the latest published
letter of Mr. John Redmond he writes—* Of course, all
we ask is autonomy in purely Irish affairs.” This seems
to me to give up the right of nationhood for the Irish
nation. No Irishman, not even Mr. John Redmond,
has a right to do this. It is inherent in the nation and
cannot be resigned to a foreign power.”” No Irishman,
according to Mr. Sweetman, has a right to ‘‘ give up the
right of nationhood,” which, according to Mr. Sweet-
man, bold, bad Mr. John Redmond, who has served a
term in jail—a place where Mr. Sweetman never recom-
mended any Irishman to go—has done; but Mr. Sweet-
man dispenses himself and pays up his Income Tax to
the British Tax-gatherer, for, if he didn’t, his furniture
would be seized ! After all, what is the inherent ‘‘right
of nationhood ’’ when placed in the balance with one’s
favourite armchair where one makes a cheap reputation
amongst boys by dreaming cheap heroics for a green
people ?

Our readers will note in our advertising columns the
- result of the Label Competition organised by Messrs.
W. J. Shaw & Sons, the well-known Bacon Firm of
Limerick. Messrs. Shaw & Sons generously increased
the number of prizes originally decided on, so that ten
additional purchasers of their puddings, who would not
under the first arrangement have received prizes, have
had the good fortune to be each presented with a fine
Irish ham as a Christmas gift. In the meantime col-
8 lectors of labels should not relax their efforts, as the firm
hope to announce further competitions for the New
Year. i

A Carrick-on-Suir reader has sent us up what he calls

a reply to the article by Catal on that town, but it is
- scarcely a reply, and is not up to publication standard.
He tells us in his covering letter that Catal’s article has
created great indignation in the breasts of some people

in Carrick, and in his reply for publication he wants to
know whyCatal writes over a pen-name rather than face
his ‘“ brother natives.”” Well, if some Carrick people
are very indignant, perhaps it is as well that Cataltook
the precaution of adopting a pen-name. Our correspon-
dent devotes a considerable amount of his ‘‘reply ”’ to
the usual class of comments and questions so familiar to
readers of controversies in local papers in Ireland. What
has Catal himself done for Carrick-on-Suir? Has he
offered to invest in the closed saw mill? Has he done
anything towards starting a bacon factory? Has he done
anything towards re-opening the woollen mills? We
read—‘‘ Liet him (Cata.)show his face to the people of

- Carrick and openly deciare that he has done so much
for their town.’”” This sort of thing is not argument
or sense, and there is no place for it in the Lrapkr.
. One thing evidently Catalhas done, he has evidently

' THE LEADER.

T

DECEMBER 28, 1907.

stirred up Carrick. Our columns are open to reasonable -
comments or replies to any of the articles in our Towns
of Ireland Series, but it is no use for indignant persons
writing up, asking what a particular writer has done to
attompt to establish some factory or other that he may .
suggest might, or should, be started. We want the.
writer of the articles on Irish towns to make as many
constructive suggestions as possible, and we certainly do
not make it a condition that before a writer points out
that a certain industry, or a certain class of activity of
any kind, is specially needed in a particular town that he
should have already attempted to realise his own sug-
gestions. So our Carrick-on-Suir correspondent, who _
calls Catal ““a moonshiner,” whatever that
is—is it a new kind of coon ?—had better give up 'bother‘
ing to know what Catal has done to re-start mills and
establish new industries. The question is—Are his re-
marks and suggestions as applied to Carrick shrewd and
reasonable ?

Controversy in Ireland is inclined to a rather low
level. ““Chanel,’” in a recent skit, which, as our readers
may remember, one young gentleman took seriously, hit
off the matter very neatly. However it may be with
other papers, we have no space for a long list of guesses
and assumptions concerning the character, shortcomings
and so forth of a writer in our columns who conceals his
identity behind a pen-name. Our correspondent sug-
gests that lectures in Irish history should be given to
certain people, and then he adds in the spirit of Irish
controversy—‘‘ I fear Catal would hardly be able to -
give them lectures on Irish history without a good }look
on the subject.”” No doubt this is calculated to wither
up the unfortunate Catat |  There is one thing we miss
in the letter we are referring to: There is no scathing
reference to ‘ this scribe,”” for, in our experience of con-
troversy as it is carried on in Ireland, the deepest and
most unutterable indignation is sounded when a writer
calls his.opponent ** this scribe.”” Why it should be so,
we cannot say, for the word ‘‘scribe ’’is a good, honest
and dignified. word.

By the way, as-we are referring to our Series on Irish
Towns, we might give a hint that our stock of articles
needs replenishing, so some of our many readers who,
we are sure, intend writing for this series might let us
have a few articles at an early date. This series so far
has been most interesting, and we feel sure that it has
at least laid the foundation of much good work and
development in many towns in Ireland.

Sergeant Francis' J. Maclaine, of the Police, has left
Glenties, in the Co. of Donegal, and ‘‘ we, the under-
signed, on behalf of the inhabitants of Glenties,’”’ gave
the policeman a gift and an illuminated address. “ We,
the undersigned,”’ are Messrs. Daniel Mulhern, J.P.;
R. H. B. Hill, J. J. McNelis, L.R.C.P. and S.I., and
Peter Gallagher, C.P.S. Policeman Maclaine suitably
replied in writing. In the course of his reply this
potentate declares: —‘ During my stay in Glenties of
almost nine years I have received nothing but the
greatest kindness and courtesy from all creeds and
classes.”” One might think it was an Attorney-General
or Solicitor-General under a ‘‘ Liberal’’ Government
who penned the following : —‘ My official duties, which,
although at times unpleasant, as might be expected
from one in my position, have been made considerably
more pleasing to me by the co-operation and assistance
of the inhabitants in my small efforts to maintain peace,
which I have endeavoured to do impartially.””  When
the extra police are being sent away from Athenry we
wonder will.they all get testimonials ¢ )

The country had better keep a sharp eye on the
 ZIndependent. It has already, with the help of the
- Irish Times and other designing factors, inflicted Hum-
‘phrey’s Dump (Failed) on this country, and the unfor-
tunate guarantors have a simply deplorable prospect to
face. The Zudependent would like the people to forget
that it forced this calamity on Ireland. In a recent
report it suppressed the reference of Mr. Orr, a director
of Messrs. Thwaites & Co., to the Exhibition. It was at
its suppressing game again in connection with the sum-
mons re the Nigger minstrels at the late Exhibition.
Mr. William Gibson, who appeared for the coloured
lady, in the course of his statement as reported in the
Irish- Temes, said that “ Mrs. Roberts was the pro-
prietress of a negro threatrical troupe of ten or more
artistes, and that her agent was a Mr. Gannon, of
Bristol. In October last she was engaged by TFisher
and a Mr. Travers through Gannon to come over here
and perform at the International Exhibition. The Con-
cert Department of the Exhibition was under the direc-
tion of Mr. McGuckin, amongst whose paid licutenants
was Travers, who, it appeared, had associated himsell
with Fisher, and they on their own account brought
over this troupe from England to perform at the Ex-
hibition. The contract was that Mrs. Roberts’ company
should be paid £30 per week. This paid official of the
Exhibition and Fisher got this company for £30 a
week, and then, according to his (counsel’s) instructions,
they proposed to lease the company to the Exhibition
for £80 for a week, thus putting £50 into their own
pockets. Counsel did not know whether anything of
that was known to Mr. McGuckin or to any of the other
officials, but it threw a good deal of light on the
tremendous loss which had fallen on the guarantors if it
was at all universal.”

The report in the Freeman of this part of counsel’s
statement is substantially the same as that in the Zrish
Times. When we turn to the Independent, that was
largely responsible “for inflicting this calamitous Ex-
hibition on the country, we find that all reference to the
alleged arrangement of charging £80 to the Exhibition
for a service that £30 was to be paid for, and in which
a paid servant of the Exhibition was alleged to be in-
volved, is eatirely suppressed. We have no particular
interest in this nigger entertainment litigation beyond
emphasising the fact of the manner in which the /nde-

WINTER DAIRYING—AND OT1HER THIKGS.

IHEARD a cynic one day observe that if the letter
I “n”” were eliminated from the alphabet the
country might have a chance of prospering. His
alliterative list was fairly extensive, including—Press,
Politicians, Parties, Patriots, Perorations, Platitudes,
Popularity, Prejudice, Police, Prisons, Potheen, Pub-
licans, Porter, Pawnbrokers, Pride and Poverty, and a
whole host of others, but though the foregoing list is
pretty comprehensive, I think if he had any wind left
he’d have puffed out another—Panacea.

The latest panacea for all Irish agricultural ailments
is Winter Dairying, or ‘“ All-the-year-round Dairying,’’
as it should more properly be called. Of course in
reading about a somewhat technical agricultural sub-
ject, the ordinary reader cannot be expected to take
sufficient interest in it, or have sufficient knowledge to
enable him to judge of its merits or demerits, and
hence he is apt to run away with the belief that if
farmers only did what they were told, the whole agri-
cultural situation would be as simple as possible, and
we’d all be happy ever after. However, I suppose
such theorists, like the poor, will be always with us, and
as they form a large proportion of that intangible but
powerful factor known as public opinion, it would be
well if they and those more directly concerned, would
take stock of a few of the radical changes which all-the-
year-round dairying involves as compared with our
present system, and so work up sufficient interest in the
subject to bring the necessary change about, if it be
thought desirable to do so.

First and foremost then the new system will mean a
return to tillage farming in those numerous places
where it has become a lost art, and increased tillage
everywhere. The question then arises—where is the
labour? It is no use now going back on ancient history
a3 to what caused labour to be scarce, but undoubtedly
the fact remains that it is scarce, and, what’s more,
there isn’t a disposition on the part of what’s there of
it to work. This is only part and parcel of the general
and perfectly conceivable feeling of unrest that has
supervened on a long period of agitation which has put
everything out of joint for the present. A farmer says,
labour won’t pay. I answer, of a kind, it won’t, but T
asked a Scotch potato grower sometime ago how many
acres he farmed? 300. How many under potatoes?
180. Where do you get the labour? From Ire-
land! Of course the rate of labour had a lot
to do with it, but if the farmers and labourers
here would only sufficiently open their eyes to
the patent fact that judicious cultivation is a
condition precedent to profitable tillage, and that in-
creased pay and employment implies better work and
attention: to an employer’s business, the foundation
would be laid for establishing a new order of things, of
which all-the-year-round dairying would be one of the
natural consequences.

Speaking of jud'icious cultivation-I mean profitable 
cropping-how many · farmers have turned to account 
the ,opening given to them by the series of tests which 
the Department conducted in England . and Scotland 
last year, and ,vhich showed that Irish-grown potatoes 
were better for seed purposes across the Channel than 
home-grown ones. 
What has this to do with all-the-
year-round dairying f 
Oh, only just that it would. have 
paid a farmer to do so. It would liave enabled lum to 
go in for it more largely next year (perhaps to get 
-his labourer to grow some of a pure seed also for ex-
portation), and it would have led to i1~creased tillage 
and increased employment, both of which are closely 
connected with the proposed new system. 
. I stated before that the new ventme will necessitate 
increased tillage. 
I might also have stated that a con-
tinuance of the present system will necessitate the same. 
Let things drift on for a few years more. 
Where are 
the milkers to come from 7 The girls are all clearing out 
of the country. The servant-boys, what's there of 'em, 
consider it 1:nfra d-1:g to milk a cow. The farmer turns 
to dry stock. 
Poor land that needs periodical turning-
up is growing furze and coarse, useless grass, and 
hungry three year-olds wander about where two crops 
of profitable animals might have been raised, and so 
the old ding-dong continues, but it won't last. 
• Let us face the situation. 
The land question is 
almost settled. The farmer won't have to face the 
probability of a landlord or land commission putting a 
tax on his improvements. 
The market is on the spot. 
The climate unequalled, the possibilities for developing 
subsidiary industries, such as poulti:y and egg-produc-
tion, unique, and still nobody seems to put his heart 
into the work. 
We seem to be all waiting for the 
Manna from Heaven, or another panacea, or another 
scapegoat on which t.o fasten our own faults. 
A comical belief seems to be pretty general amongst 
the pioneers or foll~wers of each measure of reform : that 
their own particular pet hobby is the "one and only " 
cure-all for the country's needs, as if each scheme were 
in a separate water-tight compartment of its own, 
having nothing to do or say to its neighbour. 
All-the-
year-round dairying cannot stay in its own box. It 
must have a chat with Increased Tillage next door, 
pushing the twin giants, Laziness and General Apathy, 
out of the way first, and after making friends with one 
neighbour, it must pay a call at once to another com-
partment jointly occupied by Scientific Knowledge and 
Common Sense, even though the journeY. be long and 
the blockers many. 
As the majority of your readers .are not practical 
farmers, I must nece_ssarily give only a cursory glance 
at the practical points of all-the-year-round dairying, 
my object being to arouse intelligent interest in the 
subject to the ordinary reader with a view to gradually 
working up the public favour of a reyolut~o~ary sc~eme 
that will involve a complete change m ex1stmg agncul-
tural methods, and incidentally in the country's objec-
tive--! don't, of course, mean Home Rule, in which I 
am a firm believer, but I mean that the advent of all-
the-year-round dairying and the consequent revival of 
tillage will necessitate _a return to th~ la~1d, a cha~ge 
in ·our primary-school system, substitutmg pract1:°al 
agricultural and horticultural instruction for a grmd 
for the Excise, and teaching the child from his infancy 
to · be less ashamed of farming thau counter-jumping. 
If the new system were adopted to-morrow, it woudn't 
stand half a chance. 
Feeding a cow with a ration that 
is suitable and proportionate to her _output, ho~sing ~er 
properly, breeding from the best milkers, ke~pmg milk 
and butter-fat records, and all the other simple but 
. indispensable conditions that underlie success, are not 
the common property of farmers whose previous system 
was the easy-going one involved in a few months' grass-
. feeding in the summer. 
I am afraid I cannot claim for this paper that 'it is 
very practical, or that I have confined it to one com-
partment, but I have already indicated why. T:Iowever, 
I may mention a few technical points for the ordinary 
reader. 
1. The supply of all-the-year-round butter· would 
obviate the present necessity of underselling the Dane 
. with, at least, equally good butter, as under . existing 
conditions the cross-channel grocer has to be bribed 
several shillings per cwt. to temporarily disorganise ·hfs 
trade by taking on an irregular supplier. 
2. A cow, properly fed in winter, while in milk, gets 
a second flow when the start of grass comes in April or 
.May, and has a much greater maximum yield than 
the summer-calved cow, even after due allowance is 
made for the increased cost of winter feeding. 
3. The winter-bred calf is better than the summe~ 
one, arrives at maturity quicker, and pays better in 
every way . 
These are some of the points involved, but as official 
tests are commencing this winter under the control of 
the Ulster Agricultural Society and the Co. Cork Com-
mittee of Agriculture, with a view to accurately gauging 
the relative returns from both systems, there is no 
necessity_ to enlarge on technicalities. 
This is a matter of national concern. 
If the future 
of the country depend primarily on its agriculture, ,how 
are we to look on the situation: that many of .th'e 
farmers who have purchased their holdings are only • 
availing of fixity of tenure to curtail expense by still 
further diminishing tillage. 
Under present conditions 
they are really not much to blame. 
They are only the 
victims of circumstances. 
Farmers, as a rule, are 
getting over the fanciful notion that old methods will do, 
and are getting more an..'Cious to avail of any instruction 
that will make the land yield more than at present, but 
labour is undoubtedly prohibitive under present con-
ditions. I mean that owing to a multiplicity of causes, 
a farmer at present cannot pay a 
labourer as 
much in this country as he could earn-say, in Scot-
land. These causes, chiefly the system pursued, will 
eventually be remov.able, but there is one. cause at 
present operating that seems fated to block all schemes 
of reform, and that is the disinclination of the labourer 
to work. 
He naturally concludes that the farmer, hav-
ing had a pull out of the land row, it is his turn next, 
but his peculiar plan of action is to begin by doing as 
little work as possible and making it impossible for 
the farmer to pay better wages. 
The treatment meted 
out to him in the past about labourers' cottages, and 
other causes, accounts. for a good deal of this feeling, 
but he is only "cutting his nose to spite his face." 
This c~nsideratio_n brings u_s back to bed-rock, improper. 
education, but 1t also brmgs us to the intermediate 
consideration; are all reforms in education and every-
thing else to be blocked until we get Home Rule? Are 
the innumerable crying needs of everyday life in poor 
Ireland all " red herrings across the track?" 
Must 
the farmer and the labourer drift into the belief that 
instead of having a common country and common in-
terests, they are common enemies? It seems to me, as 
an ordinary man, and in view of the problematical 
efficacy of recent methods of convincing John Bull that 
we are safe to be trusted to manaae our own affairs 
that a slight rest and a general iook around might 
1~ossibly dis_cover ~hat there are some needs more pr.es-
smg than unmediate Home Rule, and whose obtain-
m~nt would rather lead up to than retard it, and I 
thmk the so.one; the country realises this fact, the 
great?r ~he hkehhood of a vigorous Irish nation being 
at this side of the Atlantic Ocean to enjoy Home Rule 
or all-the-year-round dairying, whichever comes first.

I• HOLD that this is a question of grave importance 
• 
for Temperance Reformers-" What shall ,ve do 
with our public men?" 
By public men I mean the 
elected representatives of the }Jeople, our Aldermen, 
our Town Councillors, our Poor Law Guardians, our 
Ha1·bou'r Commissioners, our County Councillors, aud 
the rest of them. 
'.!;hey are drawn, for the most part, 
from wliat we call the middle classes, 1:.c., the better-
class farmer, the master butcher, the master builder, 
the master baker, and so on through the catalogue of 
• the trades and professions, not forgett;ing, of comse, the 
omnipotent publican. There is, indeed, amongst them 
a fairly respectable number of really temperate self-
restrained men, there is even a fair handful of total 
abstainers . . But taking cognizance of these, and making 
due allowance for them, I fear that it is true of 
au alarming number of our public representative men 
that they drink more whiskey than ,is good for them. 
It would be a crime to allege that the majority of our 
public men are drunkards. 
But it is nothing short of 
the barest truth to say that one of the fattest slices of 
that appalling annual bill of £13,000,000 comes from 
the pockets of our fairly-well:to-do intelligent public 
meri. 
Of course I do not sueak with an intimate know-
ledge of the constituent ~lements of all the Public 
_Boards of the country. 
I have au experience, however, 
extending over three c_ounties, and the remarks I pen 
being alarmingly true in these, I take it, till the 
opposite is proved for me, that they are in the main 
true of the remaining counties. 
Anyhow, 
let me 
jot down my experiences and the thoughts they suggest, 
and if I am astrai in my general assertion, I shall be 
glad to see some one enter the lists to confound me. 
Just'set your eyes upon these guardians of the public 
purse, these custodians of the public weal. 
They ai:e, 
undoubtedly, as a body, a most desirable collection of 
men. 
They possess the working brains of the country, 
they are paragons of commonsense, the soul of good 
nature, honesty and kindliness, and of patriotism, it 
goes without saying they are the " glass of fashion and 
the mould of form." But in the matter of Temperance, 
that is, of abstinence total or partial from the use of 
intoxicating drink, they are, to an alarming extent, 
public and impenitent sinners. 
The finger of the 
Temperance Reformer ],as not touched them yet. In 
certain cases which I knew it tried, it .had been burned 
with failure. 
Just watch these decent men. 
Imme-
diately that the public business of their Board is over, 
they repair in groups of two, of three, of four, or of 
five or &ix, as the case may be, to their favourite bars. 
Over and over again have I seen them describe the 
inevitable bee line. 
They go there to while away the 
time till the next train; perhaps it is mere good nature, 
the pure unadulterated but despicable sense of Irish 
sociaiity which guides them there; very often it is to 
• finish off the debates of the Board Room, to mend the 
heads that were broken, and the rents that Ca.sea ~nade 
to point their morals and adorn their ta,les. Anyhow, an 
. invisible insuperable force draws them to their favourite 
bars, with the same clockwork regularity with which the 
consciousness of their duty to the public drew them from 
their everyday business to the meeting of their public 
Board. 
They are adepts at the treating art. In fact, 
• it is part of their very essence to delight in standing a 
. drink, and equally part of it to expect from their 
guests a drink for every drink they pay for. 
Whiskey 
is ·their nectar of the gods. 
If two happen to find 
themselves together, there will be at least two solid 
drinks of whiskey employed in the process of bathing 
their lips, assuaging. their parched throats, and other-
.wise refreshing them. If there are three, there must be 
three drinks, and so on in "standing progression," till

FROM IRISH EDUCATION.

HRISTMASTIDE is a time for giving presents, 
and these presents often take the form of books. 
~ow, to ~y mind, the proper and sensible way to con-
side1: the httl~ pa.m];Jhlet which the Irish Book Company 
has Just p11bhshed 1s to look upon it and accept it as a 
m<?st va-luable a~d opportune Christmas gift to the 
Irish Nation and to the Irish race. 
This pamphlet presents to us a final verdict and judg-
ment by competent authority concerning the Irish of 
Father Peter O'Leary; and that verdict that judgment 
is favourable in the extreme. 
' 
Father' O'Leary's Irish is good, Father O'Leary's 
Irish is idiomatic, Father O'Leary's Irish is in 
continuity with 
all 
the Irish that went 
before 
it bacb to the days of Columbanus and Columcille, and 
back even beyond their day to those of Patrick and 
Bridget. 
A cheering Christmas message, linking us 
again with the Golden times of Ireland's saints and 
scholars. 
But the verdict and judgment go farther 
and declare that of all the Irish that has 
been 
composed and written and printed and published for the 
last twenty years, Father O'Leary's Irish alone forms 
the only considerable body of contemporary Gaelic litera-
ture of which these favourable things can be said. 
Such 
is the verdict, such the judgment delivered by one or 
other or all of those competent authorities-Rev. Dr. 
Henebry, Drs. Osborn Bergin, Kuno Meyer, Heinrich 
Zimmer, and Father John O'Reilly. Each has spoken 
for himself. 
The judgment of each is conclusive. 
The 
collected judgments of all are crushing. 
I, personally, am not surprised at this remarkable 
pronouncement. 
I knew long since that no other could 
be made. 
I had hardly set my foot on the shores of 
Ireland five years ago when I wrote a letter which was 
published throughout the length and breadth of the 
land, and in which-speaking of some Irish which had 
Father O'Leary's seal upon it-I said that that Irish 
was "Irish Irish." 
I meant to convey by this expres-
sion the intimation that people should be on their 
guard, that there was a great deal of spurious Irish in 
the market. And to come to more recent times I stated 
in a Lecture in Dublin, early in this year, that who-
ever 'desires to know the Irish Language must study 
Father' Peter O'Leary. 
By this statement I meant 
to convey that in the other contemporary writers and 
authors as a whole you can get either only English 
Irisli, or a crude bit of this or that dialect. 
You can-
not get-in t/iem as a whole the Irish language as a· lite-
rary vehicle of educated thought. 
. This aspect of the question has been developed at such 
length by Dr. Zimmer, Professor of Celtic Philology in 
the University of Berlin, in his letter published in the 
pamphlet under review, that for the sake of his letter 
alone the pamphlet should be procured by everyone con-

_inhabitants, lying off the Co. Antrim coast, in

diocese of the Most Rev. Dr. Henry. Readers of
THE LEADER would look in vain for it in the map of
Irish-Treland, if there were one, though practically all
the natives speak Irish ; for, notwithstanding the omni-
presence of the Gaelic League in Ireland now 12 years
or more, it was only in March of the present year that
Rathlin fell under the spell of that benign influence,
and the germs of social decay for some time apparent,
have not had time to be killed by the warmth of the
rays of this new orb of day.

Irish revivalists cannot afford to leave Rathlin ouf of
count in estimating the forces that make for progress in
the movement for regenerating the country, yet, sad to
relate, heavy obstacles are being put in the way of bring-
ing Rathlin into touch with the new life in Ireland. In
a word, it is the old, old story—the national school
belies its name and stultifies its sponsors.

The patron of this island seminary is the landlord,
Captain R. S. Gage, who resides at Bray, and the
manager is the Rev. Montgomerie Kerr, Protestant
minister, who is styled curate-in-charge. It is the only
school on the island.  The school-mistress, though re-
sident 24 years on the island and married to an Irish
speaker, neither speaks nor reads Irish, and neither she
nor. any of her five children attends the night classes.
A native speaker of Ulster Irish, trained at Cloghaneely
and duly qualified to teach the language, has been
appointed by a sub-committee of the Dial lao, and
resides on the island, doing all he can to stir up the
people and awake enthusiasm for the tongue they have
inherited from their fathers. A fair measure of suc-
cess has attended his efforts among the grown-up boys
and girls. But the children have not acquired the lan-
guage, and it is to be feared that if this solitary strong-
hold of Irish in blackest North had not been seen to
the fate of the language was doomed.

The Rev. Montgomerie Kerr, curate-in-charge, was
asked to allow Irish to be taught as an ordinary subject
during school hours. He refused, his reason being that
the children of his own flock were unwilling to learn
Irish. When it was brought home to him that neither
the children nor their parents opposed the movement
till he showed himself unfavourable to it, the curate-in-
charge replied that he could not think of allowing a
teacher of Irish to acquire influence over his young
people, and that in any case Irish was of no practical
utility. = The net result, of course, is that the Catholic
children, who are largely in the majority and who wish
to learn TIrish, are doomed to have their wishes dis-
appointed.

Meantime, the priest of the island, Father E. V.
MacGowan, "‘Adm., has not been inactive. Yet time is
passing and the language of Rathlin is fast giving way
to the tongue of the stranger. Can nothing be done?

What remains to be said is reassuring. Night classes
are held in the school for grown-up boys and girls, and
are well attended; but the curate-in-charge has effec-
tively banned them to the youth of his congregation.
The Rosary is recited in Irish in the church and in the
homes. The English titles of the Stations of the Cross

have been removed and replaced by Irish scrolls.
Mottoes in Irish gre embroidered on the altar cloths and
altar covers. Pictures of Patrick and Bridget are pro-
minent objects in the sanctuary, and Columcille will be
there, too, before long. A mission was preached in

RIVER MOODS.

HE Slaney is tidal right up to Enniscorthy Bridge,
so the cot-men who bring coal and timber and
such other freight as comes to hand lfa,ve some.what of
the seaman’s cunning in changes of wind and tide. It
is a four-hour’s journey for them down to the Wexford
docks, and cven when the tide is on the rise it tq.kes
thom half-a-day nearly to reburn. The valley is pleasant
to look on in the summer months, but now v'vhen the
river swells angrily under December storm-winds and
the fields and the woods are all bleak and bare and cold,
it is but a comfortless passage. There’s a hungry look
falls on the valley under the frown of a mld-d?,y
storm. If you stand on the road just outside Ennis-
corthy, a hundred feet more or less ab_ove the river,
you can see it curving down in great silver bands, a
quarter-mile, maybe, across, for the rains have swept it
out over the banks into the low-lying fields. The first
bend of it bosoms a deep silent pool overshadowed by
three big beeches, all gaunt and leafless now. IHere is
a rare hunting ground in golden summer days for the
little boys that hasten down from the Brothers’ sc.hoql
for their swim in the hot afternoon. At present it is
quiet and lonely, but for the stirring of an uneasy wind
in the swaying branches, and the ripple of uneasy wave-
lets on the stream. Farther down the fading glamour
of the river’s silver curves loses itself in a cloud-robed .
distance, through which you can catch faint shadows of
trees and rolling uplands, with one little bush-grown
island, and in a far measureless beyond what rises abpve
the clinging mists like a dream-range of visionary hills.
The failing light of sunset broods on it so uncertainly
that when, for a moment you look away, you turn back
thinking those dark evening clouds will have rolled
over it all, shutting out the memory of what seems
scarcely more than a dream.

If you want to see the river closer come down St.
John’s Road, up a bit of a lane that winds between high
brambly hedges to a gateway that leads to the place
where the threshers that serve half a county’s harvest
are kept; the gate is open and no one questions your
passing down and turning off into a cart track, which
flanks a thickly-wooded hill and leads you to an open
quarry where all the week-days' men labour to get
metalling for the roads. Across a style now, and over
the railroad and you stand on the right bank of the
river, looking over it to the darkening east.

There’s a great sheet of water here, all silver in the
pale evening, only with sudden troubled rufflings when
the storm gust shakes its bosom. A line of scrubby
alders hangs low over the opposite bank, which rises into
an upland of meadow and wood, with here and there a
house scarcely seen in the thickening gloom “that’s
gathering up over the slope of the East. TLook round
now, on the near bank, at the sudden sweep of hillside
where the Ring Wood, draped in its winter garments of
mist and evening-shade, rises black and gaunt against
the last twilight of a sinking day. The last sullen
glow of sunset fades and only the moon thrown a fitful
glamour between swift jagged driving clouds. The
wind has dropped, too, and only on the silence
comes the slow ooze of the sedge and one quick cry from
a frightened dab-chick splashing anxiously to her nest in
the reeds on the other side. Then there rises another
sound over the water as of a man pacing eternally up
and down a plank. Tt is a cot drawing up the river
under sail and laboriously driven pole. It passes close
to the bank, so that you see the grey face of the man
who walks slowly down the side leaning all his weight
on the pole which drives the boat up stream. It is a
black, sad-looking thing under the fitful moon gleams, :
with its one sail and the man tramping ceaselessly, down
the side, exchanging with the man at the tiller a few
words that fall strangely on the silence of the river.
But the cot soon passes out of sight, and again the river
ig left to its wild, beautiful loneliness, with only the
music in the ooze of the sedge and the low lap of the
wavelets against the banks and the wail of the night
wind beating down upon the slopes of the darkling
wood. IxAROS.

The shape was of that free, majestic mould,

Which men attained to in the days of old:

A robe of samite draped his figure tall,

Which seemed to claim no nation, yet them all:

Which might have girt a Saxon, Celt, or Gaul :

So calm T was, from very weight of grief,
That the Arch-Tempter’s self I had as lief

And soon my eyes roamed on beyond the place.
Now, deeper wilder sorrow I ’gan sup;
And, in the future, darker vistas trace;
Until I felt I had half-ebbed the cup.

And then my strength gave way, grew weak my soul;
And down my cheek unmanly tears 'gan roll ;
When on my meditations now there broke
A strange, clear voice, which all my soul awoke:
It said ‘ How long, how long enact the fool!
Your maudlin tears but further crust the yoke,
And you are but another Saxon tool.’

It was the shade that spoke. “ Look round this isle,”

It said,-“‘ there’s plenty here to weave a smile.
Come, cast off atrabilia for a space,
While I will conjure up this Celtic race;
And make its various members in review
Pass ’fore thine eyes, and featly will I trace
Their motives, aims, and conquests all for you.”’

And, as he spake, methought, across the grass,
A line of forms familiar seemed to pass:
““ Seeest thou,”” he said, ‘‘ these figures out ahead?
These are your politicians. How ill . wed!
Observe they keep no step, nor walk in tune,
And, here and there, one of their members shed,

““Dost not that little strutting red-faced man,

‘“See now advance a sunburnt, stalwart band,

Who, nathless, blindly grope to either hand;

Sore sight indeed is this to look upon,

These are the common-people—each a man,

And worthy to be called his country’s son;
But sore bewildered by each canting clan.

“ Behold, my friend,”” pursued the friendly shade,
The stuff whereof thy very self is made :
You walk through life the dupe of fool and quack, -
Who, at each turning, misdirect your track;
Your strength is lost in ever circling round;
Your sight is dimmed by ever-looking back ;
Despairing doubt your rising hopes has drown’d.”’

‘‘ Bub these,”” I cried, ‘ have been bereft of sight!

Why blame them that they do not walk aright?

O foolish thou! Seeest not around their eyes

A web is woven, of half-truths and lies? {
Each man has but to raise his strong right hand

And rend this bandage, to become full wise:

Blind-folded, but not blind this stalwart band.””

This is a people’s history of Westmeath. Mr. ‘Woods
is one of the people, and, from what I know of him,
thoroughly understands the intellect of the Midland
population. He knows that, unlike the people of the
North, South and East, the Midlanders read very
little and think less. Ie knows also that, in common
with country people elsewhere, they take miraculously-
uniting wells, and water that never boils with its fish
that never grow, for sane history. The result is: We
lack the historical sense, the sense of proportion.
Uisneach, Danish (sic) forts, round towers and Fran-
ciscan abbeys are all confused as to their origin. -Mr.
Woods has written to rectify this clouded vision. He
has done his countrymen a lasting service. Being a
living historian and ‘a well-known figure—his life was
spent amongst the people—his book will be read by
those for whom Piers, Archdall, Cogan, MacGeoghegan,
and the rest are but the names of earlier historians.
But copious extracts do his predecessors justice. Perhaps
he quotes to a fault. That would be excusable were it
not that the reader is too often denied the privilege of
enjoying Mr. Woods’ own style.

Some interesting facts in these Annals arrest the
critic’s attention. The author has not discussed them,
however. Maybe he is wise in that respect. For a
people’s history will be all the lighter without philo-
sophy. I was disappointed, however, to find no expres-
sion of opinion on the relative merits of Malachi and
Brian Boru when the Annals arrive at the death of the
former King in 1022 on Cro-Insha, Lough Ennel.

The greatest question in the mediwval ecclesiastical
life of Ireland—and doubly so for Westmeath, because
the Midlands were in communication with, and, at the
same time, outside the Pale—is whether there were two
churches in Ireland at that time, the one Celtic, the
other Norman. If so, were they hostile to one
another ?

Mr. Woods ingeniously describes the state of social
life in Westmeath during the Penal ages by reciting a
number of everyday annoyances inflicted on the Catholie
gentry of the time. THere, too, he passes over the Nor-
man and Celtic church question. The interest, of
course, lies in the query whether the Penal Laws were
directed against the Norman population of Ireland
rather than against the historic race. It is beginning
to be obvious that a slight distinction was drawn. At
the same time, the author has clearly shown that in
individual cases the Celts were as hardly dealt with
as their Norman fellow-countrymen.  But the point
should have been discussed in exztenso in the ¢ Annals of
Westmeath.”” ~ He had opportunities enough.  For,
describing Multyfarnham Abbey—an institution that
enjoys the unique distinction of having ¢ withstood the
ravages of time and the hand of the plunderer *’ from
the middle of the 13th century down to our own day—
he pays a just tribute to the monks of the so-called
‘“Dark Ages.” Further, referring to Durrow-Columb-
kille, he quotes the well-known extract from the Four
Masters concerning the death of “(1186) Hugh De
Lacy, the profaner and destroyer of many churches.’”
Yet the De Lacys were Norman Catholics! Or again,
‘“after the Anglo-Norman invasion, the monastery
founded by St. Fechin (at Fore) pined away, and its
property pased into the possession of Hugh De Lacy.”

THE LEADER.

DECEMBER 28, 1907.

I have been told that the Glens’ people are staunch
Catholics and still speak some Irish. In a fashion,
these two facts could be deduced from this volume under
review: it is very reverent in tone, contains a reference
to the Blessed Virgin, as well as purely Irish words now
and then. But the Glens’ folk have changed like the
people all through Ireland, and Moira O’Neill sorrow-
fully records it:—

‘“There’ a wheen things that used to be an’ now has
had their day,

The nine Glens of Antrim can show ye many a sight;
But not the quare wee house where we lived up Brabla’

way,

Nor a child in all the nine Glens that knows the grace

of light.”

I have quoted extensively, but have touched only a
small fraction of all the topics and subjects treated of
by the author. Every song is beautiful and fascinating.
The, at times, peculiar dialect in which they are written
is merely an added charm. One feels very much better
for having read this little volume, and having lived the
while, so to speak, amongst a deeply-religious, home-
loving and kindly-humorous people. Moore’s songs to
Chloe, Fanny Lesbia, and the rest are poor things when
campared with Moira O’Neill’s. One becomes painfully
aware of the machine-made quality of the former’s
verse after a dip into the latter’s ““ well of poetry un-
defiled.””

Poetry, it is to be feared, is a decadent art. Some
French critic has said that it will not outlast many more
generations—a couple of generations to be exact. But
while music lives, especially the music of the human
voice, songs will live. Some songs are, emphatically,
not suitable for singing, paradoxical though it may
appear. Not so with Moira O’Neill’s. T number my-
self amongst those who would have Anglo-Trish as well *
as Irish songs at our Sgopardeacca and (e iveactd;
and I venture to prophesy that any of these ballads
from the Glens of Antrim will grip an Irish audience.
Miss Nicholls sang ‘“ Sea Wrack,” set to music by
Hamilton Harty, at a feip Ceoil concert a few years
ago, and Denis O’Sullivan gave us ““ The Ould Lad ”’ at
the last peip Ceoil. I had the pleasure of listening
to both, and was delighted in each instance. =~ Why
should a foreigner recognize the beauty of a ballad from
the pen and heart of an Irishwoman, while her own
people pass coldly by?

The only fault T have to find with the book is—that
it is not big enough! We sincerely hope we shall have
many another lyric from the same writer, and hence I
say to Moira O’Neill—paopaogal cugac.
